m_Item1.QuestionText: The ________ function takes an arbitrary number of arguments and concatenates them one by one into character strings.
m_Item1.Explanation: Any numbers given among the arguments are coerced into character strings in the evident way, that is, in the same way they would be if they were printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What would be the output of the following code in R programming?
> sqrt(-17)
m_Item1.Explanation: The error message indicates that the result of the square root operation is not a number (NaN), as it is not defined for negative values in the real number system.

m_Item1.QuestionText: _______ function returns a vector of the same size as x with the elements arranged in increasing order
m_Item1.Explanation: There are other more flexible sorting facilities available like order() or sort.list() which produce a permutation to do the sorting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is used for generating sequences in R programming ?
m_Item1.Explanation: R has a number of facilities for generating commonly used sequences of numbers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the wrong statement :

Options.OptionText: Dates are represented by the Date class
Times are represented by the POSIXct or the POSIXlt class
Dates are represented by the DateTime class

m_Item1.Explanation: Dates are stored internally as the number of days since 1970-01-01 while times are stored internally as the number of seconds since 1970-01-01.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the wrong statement :

Options.OptionText: Renaming a variable in a data frame in R is surprisingly hard to do
The mutate() function exists to compute transformations of variables in a data frame
mute() function, which does the same thing as mutate() but then drops all non-transformed variables

m_Item1.Explanation: The rename() function is designed to make this process easier.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The _________ function is used to generate summary statistics from the data frame within strata defined by a variable.
m_Item1.Explanation: The general operation here is a combination of splitting a data frame into separate pieces defined by a variable or group of variables (group_by()), and then applying a summary function across those subsets (summarize()).

m_Item1.QuestionText: ________ add new variables/columns or transform existing variables in R programming
m_Item1.Explanation: In dplyr, the mutate() function is used to create new variables or modify existing variables within a data frame. It allows you to apply transformations or calculations to one or more columns and generate new values.

m_Item1.QuestionText: _________ generate summary statistics of different variables in the data frame, possibly within strata
m_Item1.Explanation: The dplyr package as a number of its own data types that it takes advantage of.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the correct statement :

Options.OptionText: Times use the POSIXct and POSIXlt class
Dates and times have special classes in R that allow for numerical and statistical calculations
Character strings can be coerced to Date/Time classes using the strptime function

m_Item1.Explanation: Character strings can be coerced to Date/Time classes using the as.Date, as.POSIXlt, or as.POSIXct.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What would be the output of the following code ? > p <- as.POSIXlt(x) > names(unclass(p)) > p$wday
m_Item1.Explanation: The POSIXlt object contains some useful metadata.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What would be the output of the following code ? > datestring <- c("January 10, 2012 10:40", "December 9, 2011 9:10") > x <- strptime(datestring, "%B %d, %Y %H:%M") > x
m_Item1.Explanation: strptime() takes a character vector that has dates and times and converts them into to a POSIXlt object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the wrong statement :

Options.OptionText: POSIXct is just a very large integer under the hood
POSIXlt stores a bunch of other useful information like the day of the week, day of the year, month, day of the month
There are a number of generic functions that work on dates and times to help you extract pieces of dates and/or times

m_Item1.Explanation: POSIXct uses a useful class when you want to store times in something like a data frame.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What would be the output of the following code ? > x <- as.Date("2012-01-01") > y <- strptime("9 Jan 2011 11:34:21", "%d%b %Y %H:%M:%S") > x-y
m_Item1.Explanation: You can use mathematical operations on dates and times.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What would be the output of the following code ? > x <- as.Date("2012-03-01") > y <- as.Date("2012-02-28") > x-y
m_Item1.Explanation: Matrix operations are also vectorized, making for nicely compact notation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What would be the output of the following code ? > x <- as.POSIXct("2012-10-25 01:00:00") > y <- as.POSIXct("2012-10-25 06:00:00", tz = "GMT") > y-x
m_Item1.Explanation: POSIXct is just a very large integer under the hood.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is multivariate version of lapply ?
m_Item1.Explanation: The mapply() function is a multivariate apply of sorts which applies a function in parallel over a set of arguments.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the correct statement :

Options.OptionText: lapply() takes elements of the list and passes them as the first argument of the function you are applying
You can use lapply() to evaluate a function multiple times each with a different argument
Functions that you pass to lapply() may have other arguments

m_Item1.Explanation: The lapply() function and its friends make heavy use of anonymous functions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: _______ is used to apply a function over subsets of a vector.
m_Item1.Explanation: t in tapply stands for table.

m_Item1.QuestionText: _______ is used to apply a function over subsets of a vector.
m_Item1.Explanation: t in tapply stands for table.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Body of lapply function is :

Options.OptionText: function (X, FUN, ...) { FUN <- match.fun(FUN) if (!is.vector(X) || is.object(X)) X <- as.list(X) .Internal(lapply(X, FUN)) }
function (X, FUN, ...) { FUN <- match.fun(FUN) if (!is.vector(X) | is.object(X)) X <- as.list(X) .Internal(lapply(X, FUN))}
function (X, FUN, ...) { FUN <- match.fun(FUN) if (is.vector(X) || is.object(X)) X <- as.list(X) .Internal(lapply(X, FUN))}

m_Item1.Explanation: Actual looping is done internally in C code for efficiency reasons.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the output of following code ? > x <- list(a = 1:5, b = rnorm(10)) > lapply(x, mean)
m_Item1.Explanation: If the original list has names, the the names will be preserved in the output.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code
? function(p) { params[!fixed] <- p mu <- params[1] sigma <- params[2]
## Calculate the Normal density a <- -0.5*length(data)*log(2*pi*sigma^2) b <- -0.5*sum((data-mu)^2) / (sigma^2) -(a + b) } > ls (environment(nLL))
m_Item1.Explanation: nLL is one of the optimization functions in R.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code ? > nLL <- make.NegLogLik(normals, c(FALSE, 2))
> optimize(nLL, c(-1, 3))$minimum
m_Item1.Explanation: optimize() function can be used rather than optim().

m_Item1.QuestionText: The _________ function is used to plot negative likelihood.
m_Item1.Explanation: For simple scatter plots, plot.default will be used.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The _________ function is a kind of "constructor function" that can be used to construct other functions.
m_Item1.Explanation: Typically, a function is defined in the global environment, so that the values of free variables are just found in the user‘s workspace.

m_Item1.QuestionText: .What will be the output of following code ? > g <- function(x) { + a <- 3 + x+a+y + ## 'y' is a free variable + } > g(2)
m_Item1.Explanation: Object =y‘ not found error is displa

m_Item1.QuestionText: ________ functions can be ?built which contain all of the necessary data for evaluating the function
m_Item1.Explanation: However, an objective function that needs to be minimized might depend on a host of other things besides its parameters.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the wrong statement :

Options.OptionText: Dynamic scoping turns out to be particularly useful for simplifying statistical computations
Lexical scoping turns out to be particularly useful for simplifying statistical computations
The scoping rules of a language determine how values are assigned to free variables

m_Item1.Explanation: Free variables are not formal arguments and are not local variables (assigned inside the function body).

m_Item1.QuestionText: The _____ function takes a vector or other objects and splits it into groups determined by a factor or list of factors.
m_Item1.Explanation: The combination of split() and a function like lapply() or sapply() is a common paradigm in R.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the output of the following code ? > f <- function(elt) { + elt[, 1] + } > lapply(x, f)
m_Item1.Explanation: Whether you use an anonymous function or you define a function first depends on your context.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the output of the following code ? > f <- function(elt) { + elt[, 1] + } > lapply(x, f)
m_Item1.Explanation: Whether you use an anonymous function or you define a function first depends on your context.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What would be the value of following expression ? > log(-2.3)
m_Item1.Explanation: This warning lets you know that taking the log of a negative number results in a NaN value because you can‘t take the log of negative numbers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What would be the value of following expression ? > log(-2.3)
m_Item1.Explanation: This warning lets you know that taking the log of a negative number results in a NaN value because you can‘t take the log of negative numbers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the value of following expression ?
m_Item1.Explanation: Warning is produced due to negative values.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the value of following expression ?
m_Item1.Explanation: Warning is produced due to negative values.

m_Item1.QuestionText: __________ prints out the function call stack after an error occurs.
m_Item1.Explanation: traceback() does nothing if there‘s no error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the wrong statement :

Options.OptionText: The primary task of debugging any R code is correctly diagnosing what the problem is
R provides only two tools to help you with debugging your code
print statement can be used for debugging purpose

m_Item1.Explanation: R provides a number of tools to help you with debugging your code.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the correct statement :

Options.OptionText: The traceback() function must be called immediately after an error occurs
The debugger calls the browser at the very low level of the function body
Every time you call the mod() function it will launch the interactive debugger

m_Item1.Explanation: Once another function is called, you lose the traceback.

m_Item1.QuestionText: _______ allows you to modify the error behavior so that you can browse the function call stack
m_Item1.Explanation: The recover() function can be used to modify the error behavior of R when an error occurs.

m_Item1.QuestionText: ______ suspends the execution of a function wherever it is called and puts the function in debug mode
m_Item1.Explanation: The debug() function initiates an interactive debugger (also known as the ?browser? in R) for a function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: ______ suspends the execution of a function wherever it is called and puts the function in debug mode
m_Item1.Explanation: The debug() function initiates an interactive debugger (also known as the ?browser? in R) for a function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What would be the output of the following code ? > mean(x) Error in mean(x) : object 'x' not found > traceback()
m_Item1.Explanation: The traceback() function shows you how many levels deep you were when the error occurred.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The recover() function will first print out the function call stack when an _______ occurs.
m_Item1.Explanation: When you choose a frame number, you will be put in the browse and will have the ability to poke around.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The recover() function will first print out the function call stack when an _______ occurs.
m_Item1.Explanation: When you choose a frame number, you will be put in the browse and will have the ability to poke around.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the correct statement :

Options.OptionText: In R, a function is an object which has the mode function
R interpreter is able to pass control to the function, along with arguments that may be necessary for the function to accomplish the actions that are desired
Functions are also often written when code must be shared with others or the public

m_Item1.Explanation: An argument list is a comma separated list of formal arguments.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The __________ function returns a list of all the formal arguments of a function
m_Item1.Explanation: Functions have named arguments which can optionally have default values.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code ?
> f <- function(num = 1) { + hello <- "Hello, world! " + for(i in seq_len(num)) { + cat(hello) + } + chars <- nchar(hello) * num + chars + } > f()
m_Item1.Explanation: The formal arguments are the arguments included in the function definition.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the wrong statement :

Options.OptionText: A formal argument can be a symbol, a statement of the form "symbol = expression‘, or the special formal argument
The first component of the function declaration is the keyword function
The value returned by the call to function is not a function

m_Item1.Explanation: The value returned by the call to function is a function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: You can check to see whether an R object is NULL with the _________ function.
m_Item1.Explanation: It is sometimes useful to allow an argument to take the NULL value, which might indicate that the function should take some specific action.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following code will print NULL ?
m_Item1.Explanation: One catch with … is that any arguments that appear after … on the argument list must be named explicitly and cannot be partially matched or matched positionally.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the output of following code ? > f <- function(a, b) { + a^2 + } > f(2)
m_Item1.Explanation: This function never actually uses the argument b, so calling f(2) will not produce an error because the 2 gets positionally matched to a.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the output of following code ? > f <- function(a, b) { + print(a) + print(b) + } > f(45)
m_Item1.Explanation: Arguments to functions are evaluated lazily, so they are evaluated only as needed in the body of the function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: _________ is an indication that a fatal problem has occurred and execution of the function stops
m_Item1.Explanation: Errors are produced by the stop() function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the correct statement :

Options.OptionText: R has a number of ways to indicate to you that something‘s not right
Executing any function in R may result in the condition
"condition" is a generic concept for indicating that something unexpected has occurred

m_Item1.Explanation: Programmers can create their own custom conditions if they want.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What would be the value of following expression ? log(-1)
m_Item1.Explanation: This warning lets you know that taking the log of a negative number results in a NaN value because you can‘t take the log of negative numbers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Warnings are generated by the _________ function
m_Item1.Explanation: warning is an indication that something is wrong but not necessarily fatal; execution of the function continues.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code ?
> printmessage <- function(x) { + if(x > 0)
+ print("x is greater than zero") + else + print("x is less than or equal to zero") + invisible(x) + } > printmessage(1)
m_Item1.Explanation: The function seems to work fine. No errors, warnings, or messages.

m_Item1.QuestionText: To get the current date, the _______ function will return a Date object which can be converted to a different class if necessary.
m_Item1.Explanation: The POSIXlt class stores date/time values as a list of components (hour, min, sec, mon, etc.) making it easy to extract these parts.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What would be the output of the following code ? > x <- as.Date("1970-01-01") > x
m_Item1.Explanation: Dates are represented by the Date class and can be coerced from a character string using the as.Date() function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the output of following code snippet ? > lm <- function(x) { x * x } > lm
m_Item1.Explanation: When R tries to bind a value to a symbol, it searches through a series of environments to find the appropriate value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the correct statement :

Options.OptionText: The search list can be found by using the searchlist() function
The search list can be found by using the search() function
The global environment or the user‘s workspace is always the second element of the search list

m_Item1.Explanation: Base package is always the last element.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A function, together with an environment, makes up what is called a ______ closure.
m_Item1.Explanation: The function closure model can be used to create functions that "carry around" data with them.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the variable in the following code is variable ? > f <- function(x, y) { + x^2 + y / z + }
m_Item1.Explanation: This function has 2 formal arguments x and y. In the body of the function there is another symbol z. In this case z is called a free variable.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the wrong statement :

Options.OptionText: The order of the packages on the search list does not matter
R has separate namespaces for functions and non-functions
Users can configure which packages get loaded on startup so if you are writing a function

m_Item1.Explanation: The order of the packages on the search list matters, particularly if there are multiple objects with the same name in different packages.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is apply function in R ?
m_Item1.Explanation: Functions can be passed as arguments to other functions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the correct statement :

Options.OptionText: Writing functions is a core activity of an R programmer
Functions are often used to encapsulate a sequence of expressions that need to be executed numerous times
Functions are also often written when code must be shared with others or the public

m_Item1.Explanation:
It represents the key step of the transition from a mere ?user? to a developer who creates new
functionality for R.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Functions are defined using the _________ directive and are stored as R objects
m_Item1.Explanation: In particular, they are R objects of class "function"

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code ?
> f <- function() { + ## This is an empty function + }
> f()
m_Item1.Explanation: This function takes no arguments and does nothing.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the wrong statement :

Options.OptionText: Functions in R are "second class objects"
The writing of a function allows a developer to create an interface to the code, that is explicitly specified with a set of parameters
Functions provides an abstraction of the code to potential users

m_Item1.Explanation: Functions in R are "first class objects", which means that they can be treated much like any other R object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the output of the following code ? > f <- function() { + ## This is an empty function + } > class(f)
m_Item1.Explanation: Functions have their own class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following code will print "Hello, world!" in R Programming?
m_Item1.Explanation: Please see print statement carefully.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of following code in R programming ?
> f <- function(num)
{ 
for(i in seq_len(num))
{
cat("Hello, world! ") 
} 
}
> f(3)

Options.OptionText: Hello, world! Hello, world! Hello, world!

m_Item1.Explanation:
defines a function f() that takes a single argument num. Within the function, a for loop is used to iterate i from 1 to num. Inside the loop, the cat() function is used to print the string "Hello, world!" to the console.

If you call the function f() with an argument, for example, f(3), it will execute the loop three times and print "Hello, world!" three times.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of following code ?
> f <- function(num) { + hello <- "Hello, world! " + for(i in seq_len(num)) { + cat(hello) + } + chars <- nchar(hello) * num + chars + } > meaningoflife <- f(3) > print(meaningoflife)
m_Item1.Explanation: This function returns the total number of characters printed to the console.

m_Item1.QuestionText: ______ evaluate the cumulative distribution function for a Normal distribution
m_Item1.Explanation: p stands for cumulative distribution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the wrong statement :

Options.OptionText: For each probability distribution there are typically three functions
For each probability distribution there are typically four functions
r function is sufficient for simulating random numbers

m_Item1.Explanation: There are typically four functions available that start with a "r", "d", "p", and "q".

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the wrong statement :

Options.OptionText: For each probability distribution there are typically three functions
For each probability distribution there are typically four functions
r function is sufficient for simulating random numbers

m_Item1.Explanation: There are typically four functions available that start with a "r", "d", "p", and "q".

m_Item1.QuestionText: _________ is the most common probability distribution to work with.
m_Item1.Explanation: Working with the Normal distributions requires using four functions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: _________ is the most common probability distribution to work with.
m_Item1.Explanation: Working with the Normal distributions requires using four functions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the output of the following code ? > pnorm(2)
m_Item1.Explanation: If you wanted to know what was the probability of a random Normal variable of being less than 2, you could use the pnorm() function to do that calculation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the output of the following code ? > pnorm(2)
m_Item1.Explanation: If you wanted to know what was the probability of a random Normal variable of being less than 2, you could use the pnorm() function to do that calculation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: 5 Normal random numbers can be generated with rnorm() by setting seed value to :
m_Item1.Explanation: set.seed(1) will give 5 normal random numbers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: 5 Normal random numbers can be generated with rnorm() by setting seed value to :
m_Item1.Explanation: set.seed(1) will give 5 normal random numbers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the wrong statement :

Options.OptionText: Drawing samples from specific probability distributions can be done with "s" functions
The sample() function draws randomly from a specified set of (scalar) objects allowing you to sample from arbitrary distributions of numbers
The sampling() function draws randomly from a specified set of objects

m_Item1.Explanation: The sample() function can be used to draw random samples from arbitrary vectors.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the wrong statement :

Options.OptionText: Drawing samples from specific probability distributions can be done with "s" functions
The sample() function draws randomly from a specified set of (scalar) objects allowing you to sample from arbitrary distributions of numbers
The sampling() function draws randomly from a specified set of objects

m_Item1.Explanation: The sample() function can be used to draw random samples from arbitrary vectors.

m_Item1.QuestionText: __________ distribution is commonly used to model data that come in the form of counts.
m_Item1.Explanation: It is possible to generate random numbers from other probability distributions like the Poisson.

m_Item1.QuestionText: __________ distribution is commonly used to model data that come in the form of counts.
m_Item1.Explanation: It is possible to generate random numbers from other probability distributions like the Poisson.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following code represents count with mean of 2 ?
m_Item1.Explanation: rpois(10, 20) give counts with a mean of 20.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following code represents count with mean of 2 ?
m_Item1.Explanation: rpois(10, 20) give counts with a mean of 20.

m_Item1.QuestionText: ________ is a systematic way to examine how much time is spent in different parts of a program.
m_Item1.Explanation: Sometimes profiling becomes necessary as a project grows and layers of code are placed on top of each other.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the correct statement :

Options.OptionText: Rprof() is used to turn off the profiler
At each line of the output, the profiler writes out the function call stack
The summaryprof() function tabulates the R profiler output

m_Item1.Explanation: The Rprofiler() function starts the profiler in R.

m_Item1.QuestionText: _______ divides the time spend in each function by the total run time
m_Item1.Explanation: "by.self" does the same as "by.total".

m_Item1.QuestionText: _______ divides the time spend in each function by the total run time
m_Item1.Explanation: "by.self" does the same as "by.total".

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following function actually fits the linear model ?
m_Item1.Explanation: lm() function does a bit of pre-processing and checking before it actually fits the model.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following function actually fits the linear model ?
m_Item1.Explanation: lm() function does a bit of pre-processing and checking before it actually fits the model.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statement gives sampling interval ?
m_Item1.Explanation: Sampling time is expressed in secs.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a valid way to define a user-defined function in R?
m_Item1.Explanation: In R, a function is defined using the syntax: function_name <- function(parameters) {body}. Thus, option C correctly uses this syntax to define a user-defined function.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will the following function call return: my_sum(a = 2, b = 3) given the function definition:
my_sum <- function(a, b = 4)
{
a + b
} ?
m_Item1.Explanation: The function my_sum has a default parameter b = 4. However, when calling my_sum(a = 2, b = 3), the value of b is overridden to 3. So, the return value will be 2 + 3 = 5.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following indicates a correct way to pass arguments by reference in R?

Options.OptionText: R doesn't support passing by reference.
Use the '&' symbol before the variable.
Wrap the variable in a function call.

m_Item1.Explanation: R does not natively support passing arguments by reference. All arguments in R are passed by value, meaning a copy of the variable is created within the function.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following code:
multiply <- function(x, y)
{
    return(x * y)
}
What will 'multiply(4)' return?
m_Item1.Explanation: The function 'multiply' requires two arguments. The call 'multiply(4)' provides only one, resulting in an error due to missing the second argument.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of R functions, what is 'lazy evaluation'?

Options.OptionText: Evaluating all arguments upon function call
Deferring function execution until a condition is met
Evaluating arguments only when they are actually used
Using default values for all function arguments

m_Item1.Explanation: Lazy evaluation in R means that function arguments are only evaluated when they are actually used within the function body, rather than at the point of function call.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R is used to combine multiple vectors into a list?
m_Item1.Explanation: The list() function in R is used to combine multiple vectors into a list.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following functions is used to apply a function over a list or vector in R?
m_Item1.Explanation: lapply() is used to apply a function over a list or a vector, returning a list in R.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the function seq() generate in R?
m_Item1.Explanation: The seq() function is used to generate regular sequences of numbers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R can be used to compute the factorial of a number?
m_Item1.Explanation: The factorial() function is used to calculate the factorial of a number.

m_Item1.QuestionText: To concatenate strings in R, which function should you use?
m_Item1.Explanation: The paste() function is used for concatenating strings in R.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R produces a vector of identical numbers of a specified length?
m_Item1.Explanation: The rep() function is used to produce a vector of identical numbers of specified length.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary use of the sink() function in R?

Options.OptionText: To read data from a file
To save output to a file

m_Item1.Explanation: The sink() function is used to redirect the R output to a file.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R calculates the distance between pairs of a set of objects?
m_Item1.Explanation: The dist() function is used to calculate the distance between pairs of a set of objects in R.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R returns the rank of each value in a vector?
m_Item1.Explanation: The rank() function returns the rank of each value in a vector.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R would you use to round numbers to the specified number of decimal places?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'round' function rounds the values to the specified number of decimal places.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following functions in R is used to calculate the absolute value of a number?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'abs' function returns the absolute (positive) value of a given number.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the 'floor' function in R do to the given numeric value?

Options.OptionText: Rounds it up to the nearest integer
Rounds it down to the nearest integer
Removes the decimal part without rounding

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'floor' function rounds the given numeric value down to the nearest integer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you find the largest integer less than or equal to a given number in R?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'floor' function returns the largest integer less than or equal to the given value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R returns the smallest integer greater than or equal to a number?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'ceiling' function in R returns the smallest integer greater than or equal to a specified number.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which numeric function would you use in R to find the exponential of a given number?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'exp' function calculates the exponential (e^x) of the specified number.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In R, which function would you use to extract the integer part of a numeric value?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'trunc' function removes the fractional part of a numeric value, leaving only the integer part.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function would you use in R to find the square root of a number?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'sqrt' function is used in R to compute the square root of a given number.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R is used to concatenate strings together?
m_Item1.Explanation: The paste() function in R is used to concatenate strings together with a specified separator.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the nchar() function return when applied to a character string?
m_Item1.Explanation: nchar() returns the length of a string, i.e., the number of characters present in the string.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function would you use to extract a substring from a string in R?
m_Item1.Explanation: substr() is used to extract or replace substrings in a character vector.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the toupper() function in R?
m_Item1.Explanation: toupper() is used to convert all characters in a string to uppercase.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How do you split a string into words in R using a specified delimiter?
m_Item1.Explanation: strsplit() splits strings into substrings based on a specified delimiter.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R substitutes all occurrences of a pattern in a string?
m_Item1.Explanation: gsub() replaces all occurrences of a pattern in a string with a replacement.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you convert a character string to lowercase in R?
m_Item1.Explanation: tolower() converts all characters in a string to lowercase.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the trimws() function do when applied to a character string in R?
m_Item1.Explanation: trimws() removes leading and trailing whitespace from a character string.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R is used to calculate the median of a numeric vector?
m_Item1.Explanation: The median() function is used to compute the median of a numeric vector in R.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'sd()' function in R?
m_Item1.Explanation: The sd() function in R is used to calculate the standard deviation of a numeric vector.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R returns the interquartile range of a dataset?
m_Item1.Explanation: The IQR() function is used to calculate the interquartile range of a dataset in R.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function is used in R to generate a summary of statistics for a data object?
m_Item1.Explanation: The summary() function provides a brief summary, including statistical measures for a given R object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How do you calculate the variance of a numeric vector in R?
m_Item1.Explanation: The var() function computes the variance of a numeric vector in R.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the 'quantile()' function in R return when used on a numeric vector?

Options.OptionText: Five values representing the entire range
Quantile values based on specified probabilities
The most frequent values in a dataset

m_Item1.Explanation: The quantile() function returns the quantiles of a numeric vector based on the specified probabilities.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R calculates the correlation between two variables?
m_Item1.Explanation: The cor() function in R computes the correlation coefficient between two variables.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the output of the 'apply()' function when used with the argument 'FUN = mean' on a data frame?

Options.OptionText: The overall mean of the data frame

m_Item1.Explanation: When apply() is used with 'FUN = mean' on a data frame, it calculates the mean of each column by default.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of using R Markdown in data analysis?

Options.OptionText: To interactively change data without reproducibility
To draft static reports with no dynamic capabilities
To create dynamic documents that integrate code, output, and narrative
To compile R scripts into an R script file

m_Item1.Explanation: R Markdown allows the integration of code, output, and written analysis in a single document, making it ideal for dynamic and interactive reports that are reproducible and shareable.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in R would you use to convert an R Markdown file to an HTML document?
m_Item1.Explanation: The render() function from the 'rmarkdown' package is used to convert R Markdown files into different formats including HTML.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you include a plot in an R Markdown document?

Options.OptionText: Save the plot externally and import it
Directly include the plotting code in an R code chunk
Convert the plot to a table and include it

m_Item1.Explanation: You can include plots directly by writing the plotting code inside an R code chunk. The plot will be generated and displayed as part of the document.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which YAML option in the header is necessary to specify that your R Markdown output should be an interactive HTML document?
m_Item1.Explanation: The yaml header in R Markdown should specify 'output: html_document' for an interactive HTML document output.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'knitr::kable()' function in R Markdown?

Options.OptionText: To transform R code into Python code
To improve the speed of document rendering
To create nicely formatted tables within the document
To generate bar plots from table data

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'knitr::kable()' function is used to create appealing and well-formatted tables within an R Markdown document, enhancing readability and presentation.
Once the call to lapply() is finished, the function disappears and does not appear in the workspace.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Point out the correct statement :

Options.OptionText: Rprofiler() tabulates how much time is spent inside each function
Rprof() keeps track of the function call stack at regularly sampled intervals
By default, the profiler samples the function call stack every 2 seconds

m_Item1.Explanation: The profiler is started by calling the Rprof() function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: system.time function returns an object of class _______ which contains two useful bits of information.
m_Item1.Explanation: if there‘s an error, gives the time until the error occurred.

m_Item1.QuestionText: system.time function returns an object of class _______ which contains two useful bits of information.
m_Item1.Explanation: if there‘s an error, gives the time until the error occurred.
